title: Acid-Base Chemistry and pH
subject: AP Chemistry
objectives: Define acids and bases using both the Arrhenius and Br√łnsted-Lowry models
Calculate pH and pOH from hydrogen ion concentration and vice versa
Distinguish between strong and weak acids/bases and explain Ka as a measure of acid strength

sections.heading: Two Models for Acids and Bases
sections.content:
The Arrhenius model defines acids as substances that produce H‚Āļ ions in water and bases as substances that produce OH‚ĀĽ ions. HCl ‚Üí H‚Āļ + Cl‚ĀĽ (acid). NaOH ‚Üí Na‚Āļ + OH‚ĀĽ (base).

The Br√łnsted-Lowry model is broader: an acid is a proton (H‚Āļ) donor, and a base is a proton acceptor. This model works even without water. In the reaction NH‚āÉ + H‚āāO ‚áĆ NH‚āĄ‚Āļ + OH‚ĀĽ, water donates a proton to ammonia ‚ÄĒ water acts as the acid, ammonia acts as the base.

Every Br√łnsted-Lowry acid-base reaction involves conjugate pairs. When HCl donates H‚Āļ, it becomes Cl‚ĀĽ (its conjugate base). When NH‚āÉ accepts H‚Āļ, it becomes NH‚āĄ‚Āļ (its conjugate acid). The stronger an acid, the weaker its conjugate base.

sections.heading: The pH Scale
sections.content:
pH = ‚ąílog[H‚Āļ]    and    [H‚Āļ] = 10^(‚ąípH)
pOH = ‚ąílog[OH‚ĀĽ]   and    [OH‚ĀĽ] = 10^(‚ąípOH)
pH + pOH = 14     (at 25¬įC)

The ion product of water: Kw = [H‚Āļ][OH‚ĀĽ] = 1.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī at 25¬įC

Examples:
  [H‚Āļ] = 1.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬≥ M ‚Üí pH = 3.0 (acidic)
  [H‚Āļ] = 1.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚Ā∑ M ‚Üí pH = 7.0 (neutral)
  [H‚Āļ] = 1.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬≤ M ‚Üí pH = 12.0 (basic)

pH < 7: acidic    pH = 7: neutral    pH > 7: basic

The pH scale is logarithmic: a difference of 1 pH unit means a 10-fold change in [H‚Āļ]. Stomach acid (pH ~2) is 100,000 times more acidic than pure water (pH 7).

sections.heading: Strong vs. Weak Acids and Bases
sections.content:
Strong acids ionize completely in water. There are six to memorize: HCl, HBr, HI, HNO‚āÉ, H‚āāSO‚āĄ, and HClO‚āĄ. If you dissolve 0.10 M HCl, you get 0.10 M H‚Āļ and 0.10 M Cl‚ĀĽ ‚ÄĒ no intact HCl molecules remain. pH = ‚ąílog(0.10) = 1.0.

Weak acids ionize only partially. Acetic acid (CH‚āÉCOOH) in water establishes an equilibrium:
CH‚āÉCOOH ‚áĆ CH‚āÉCOO‚ĀĽ + H‚Āļ, Ka = 1.8 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚ĀĶ

Ka (acid dissociation constant) measures acid strength. A larger Ka means more ionization, stronger acid. Ka for HCl is essentially infinite (complete ionization); Ka for acetic acid is tiny (very little ionization).

Strong bases (NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)‚āā) dissociate completely. Weak bases (like NH‚āÉ) only partially accept protons:
NH‚āÉ + H‚āāO ‚áĆ NH‚āĄ‚Āļ + OH‚ĀĽ, Kb = 1.8 √ó 10‚ĀĽ‚ĀĶ

pH = ‚ąílog[H‚Āļ]. Strong acids/bases ionize completely; weak acids/bases establish equilibrium. Ka measures acid strength: larger Ka = stronger acid. For any conjugate pair, Ka √ó Kb = Kw = 1.0 √ó 10‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚Āī.

sections.heading: Buffers: Resisting pH Change
sections.content:
A buffer is a solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added. Buffers consist of a weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid) in roughly equal concentrations.

Example: An acetic acid/sodium acetate buffer (CH‚āÉCOOH / CH‚āÉCOO‚ĀĽ).

If you add H‚Āļ: The conjugate base (CH‚āÉCOO‚ĀĽ) reacts with it: CH‚āÉCOO‚ĀĽ + H‚Āļ ‚Üí CH‚āÉCOOH. The H‚Āļ is consumed, pH barely changes.

If you add OH‚ĀĽ: The weak acid reacts with it: CH‚āÉCOOH + OH‚ĀĽ ‚Üí CH‚āÉCOO‚ĀĽ + H‚āāO. The OH‚ĀĽ is consumed, pH barely changes.

The Henderson-Hasselbalch equation gives the pH of a buffer:
pH = pKa + log([A‚ĀĽ]/[HA])

where pKa = ‚ąílog(Ka), [A‚ĀĽ] is the conjugate base concentration, and [HA] is the weak acid concentration. When [A‚ĀĽ] = [HA], pH = pKa.

Buffers are essential in biology ‚ÄĒ your blood is buffered at pH 7.4 by the carbonic acid/bicarbonate system (H‚āāCO‚āÉ/HCO‚āÉ‚ĀĽ). A change of even 0.2 pH units can be life-threatening. Understanding buffers connects acid-base chemistry directly to physiology.

aiContext: AP Chemistry. Use dimensional analysis for all calculations. Stress WHY reactions happen, not just equation balancing. Common confusion: entropy vs enthalpy direction, and applying Le Chatelier to specific perturbations.
